rationale_for_baseline_tier: Although earlobe sparing strongly favours a relapsing polychondritis flare, an acutely red, hot, swollen ear where bacterial perichondritis (threatening cartilage necrosis) and mastoiditis cannot be excluded remotely warrants urgent same/next-day in-person assessment — a T3 baseline — with interim NSAIDs/cold compress, escalating to T4 (ED) for spreading/lobe involvement, post-auricular signs, fever, or airway symptoms.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness spreads to the face/neck or involves the earlobe, swelling/pain behind the ear with ear protrusion, canal discharge, or fever
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests bacterial perichondritis/mastoiditis needing IV antibiotics ± drainage.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness spreads to your face/neck or the earlobe, you get swelling/pain behind the ear with the ear pushed forward, discharge, or a fever, go to the emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Hoarseness, pain over the windpipe, or any breathing difficulty
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests laryngotracheal relapsing polychondritis threatening the airway.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you become hoarse, get pain over your windpipe, or have any trouble breathing, call 000 immediately.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Maintain an airway safety-net and ensure rheumatology linkage.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Because only the firm cartilage part of your ear is inflamed and your soft earlobe is normal, this is most likely your cartilage-inflammation condition, not a spreading infection. Take an anti-inflammatory with food, use a cold compress (no heat), and sleep on the other side — but because a cartilage infection can't be fully ruled out over video, be seen in person today or first thing tomorrow. Go to ED if the redness spreads to your face/neck or earlobe, you get swelling behind the ear, a fever, or any hoarseness or trouble breathing (call 000 for breathing trouble).
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Urgent same/next-day in-person assessment (GP/ENT); ED/000 for red flags
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Attend ED tonight if infection spreads or airway symptoms develop.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth review of an acute auricular flare with earlobe sparing (likely relapsing polychondritis); bacterial perichondritis/mastoiditis not excludable remotely; urgent in-person assessment advised; NSAID/cold compress interim; mastoiditis/airway safety-net given.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reassuring home-only on the basis of earlobe sparing without arranging in-person assessment to exclude cartilage infection.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A co-existing bacterial perichondritis is missed; the cartilage becomes necrotic (cauliflower ear), or an evolving mastoiditis spreads intracranially.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: T3 is correct; risk is failing to escalate on infection/airway features.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Ambulance without airway/systemic features is over-triage; urgent same/next-day assessment (T3) or ED (T4) suffices.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Cannot palpate the mastoid, perform otoscopy, or definitively exclude bacterial perichondritis remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Recognise earlobe sparing but arrange urgent in-person assessment to exclude cartilage infection, with interim NSAIDs and a mastoiditis/airway safety-net.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Reassure home-only as 'just a flare' without in-person assessment.
